topic.prompt_en: Describe an experience you still remember clearly.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to make a memorable experience vivid without becoming dramatic or essay-like.

angles.title: Set the scene
content: Say when it happened and why it mattered at the time.
Keep the background short so you can move into the event quickly.

angles.title: Explain what happened
content: Describe the main action in a clear time order.
Choose one or two details that make the event believable.

angles.title: Show the turning point
content: Include the moment when the situation changed or became clear.
That moment usually carries the emotional weight of the answer.

angles.title: End with meaning
content: Finish with the lesson, result, or lasting impression.
A short reflection gives the story a real ending.

sequence.target: State the situation, time, and why this event belongs in your answer.
Describe what happened and where the key shift came.
End with the effect, lesson, or memory that stayed with you.

high_score_tips: Keep the event focused on one clear thread instead of listing everything that happened.
Use one turning point so the answer has shape and forward movement.
A calm reflection usually sounds stronger than exaggerated emotion.
Let the ending show why the event still matters to you now.

content.why_it_hurts: The listener loses the main story if every small detail gets equal time.
content.fix: Keep only the details that change the meaning or the outcome.

content.why_it_hurts: Without a shift, the answer sounds like a flat report instead of a real experience.
content.fix: Identify the exact moment when something became easier, harder, clearer, or more memorable.

content.why_it_hurts: The story feels unfinished if you stop right after the event ends.
content.fix: Add one sentence about what you learned or how you see it now.

language.wrong_example: I go there last week and everyone is waiting for me.
language.better_example: I went there last week and everyone was waiting for me.
language.reason: When you tell a finished event, simple past should carry the main timeline.

language.wrong_example: It was very memorable and very special.
language.better_example: It was memorable because it changed how I handled similar situations later.
language.reason: Specific explanation is stronger than repeating very.

core.example: I would naturally use "incident" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "outcome" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "detail" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "reaction" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "volunteer" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "hospitality" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful when the story changes direction.

core.example: I would use "what happened was" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "at first" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "in the end" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "it taught me that" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "stick in my mind" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "be struck by" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.

extended.phrase: one thing that stood out was
extended.note: Good for the moment you want to emphasize.
extended.example: I would use "one thing that stood out was" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.

extended.note: Useful when the event caused a later change.
extended.example: I would use "from that point on" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "not flashy at all" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would use "leave a lasting impression" when describing an experience that stayed with me for years.

framework.goal: Tell one event clearly through context, turning point, result, and reflection.

moves: Set the time and situation in one or two sentences.
Show why this event was worth talking about.
Make the turning point obvious and easy to follow.
State the outcome and what stayed with you.
Keep the reflection grounded and realistic.

delivery_markers: The moment that really stood out was ...
At first, I thought ... but then ...

samples.answer:
An experience that stayed with me for years is something I can talk about quite naturally. A few years ago, I joined a small volunteer trip to a mountain village during my university summer break. The work itself was simple, but the pace of life there was completely different from what I was used to. The moment that stayed with me was having dinner with a local family and realising how warm people can be without trying to impress anyone. I came back physically tired but mentally much lighter, and I kept thinking about that trip for weeks.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. It reminded me that meaningful experiences are often quiet and ordinary rather than exciting on the surface. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 描述一次难忘经历 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is an experience that stayed with me for years.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: It mattered because It reminded me that meaningful experiences are often quiet and ordinary rather than exciting on the surface.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: The moment that stayed with me was having dinner with a local family and realising how warm people can be without trying to impress anyone.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is an experience that stayed with me for years. A few years ago, I joined a small volunteer trip to a mountain village during my university summer break. The work itself was simple, but the pace of life there was completely different from what I was used to. What stood out most was that the moment that stayed with me was having dinner with a local family and realising how warm people can be without trying to impress anyone.

After that, i came back physically tired but mentally much lighter, and I kept thinking about that trip for weeks. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. It reminded me that meaningful experiences are often quiet and ordinary rather than exciting on the surface. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.

Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with an experience that stayed with me for years. A few years ago, I joined a small volunteer trip to a mountain village during my university summer break. The work itself was simple, but the pace of life there was completely different from what I was used to. The turning point, for me, was that the moment that stayed with me was having dinner with a local family and realising how warm people can be without trying to impress anyone.

From there, i came back physically tired but mentally much lighter, and I kept thinking about that trip for weeks. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. It reminded me that meaningful experiences are often quiet and ordinary rather than exciting on the surface. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.

practice.task: Describe an experience you still remember clearly.
checklist: Set the scene briefly before the main event.
End with the result or lesson, not just the action.
self_prompts: What changed in the middle of the story?
Why do you still remember it now?
What did you learn from it?
